name: Transformer Architecture
description: Core transformer architecture including self-attention, positional encoding, and encoder-decoder structures used in LLMs
key_facts: Transformers use self-attention mechanisms to weigh the importance of different tokens in a sequence
Positional encodings are added to input embeddings since transformers have no inherent sense of token order
Decoder-only architectures (like GPT) generate text autoregressively, predicting one token at a time
Multi-head attention allows the model to attend to information from different representation subspaces simultaneously
Layer normalization and residual connections stabilize training in deep transformer networks
common_misconceptions: Transformers process tokens sequentially like RNNs (they process all tokens in parallel during training)
More attention heads always means better performance (there are diminishing returns and computational trade-offs)

name: Tokenization and Embeddings
description: Methods for converting text into numerical representations for LLM processing
key_facts: Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) iteratively merges the most frequent pairs of characters or subwords to build a vocabulary
SentencePiece is a language-independent tokenizer used by many LLMs including LLaMA and T5
Token embeddings map discrete tokens to dense continuous vector representations
Vocabulary size affects model size, training speed, and ability to handle rare words
WordPiece tokenization is used by BERT and splits unknown words into known subword units
common_misconceptions: Each word always corresponds to exactly one token (common words may be one token, but many words are split into multiple subword tokens)
Larger vocabularies are always better (they increase embedding table size and can reduce training efficiency)

name: LLM Pre-training
description: Pre-training objectives, data requirements, and scaling laws for large language models
key_facts: Causal language modeling (next-token prediction) is the primary pre-training objective for decoder-only models
Masked language modeling (MLM) is used by encoder models like BERT to predict randomly masked tokens
Scaling laws show predictable relationships between model size, dataset size, compute, and loss
Pre-training typically requires large diverse text corpora spanning web data, books, and code
NVIDIA NeMo Framework provides tools for large-scale LLM pre-training on multi-GPU and multi-node setups
common_misconceptions: Pre-training teaches a model specific tasks (it learns general language patterns; task-specific behavior comes from fine-tuning or prompting)
Larger models always perform better (performance depends on training data quality and compute budget, not just parameter count)

name: Prompt Engineering Fundamentals
description: Core techniques for crafting effective prompts including zero-shot, few-shot, and chain-of-thought prompting
key_facts: Zero-shot prompting provides only a task description without examples
Few-shot prompting includes example input-output pairs to guide the model's response format
Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting encourages step-by-step reasoning for complex tasks
System prompts set the overall behavior and persona for chat-based LLMs
Temperature controls randomness in token selection: lower values produce more deterministic outputs
common_misconceptions: More examples in few-shot prompts always improve results (too many examples can exceed context length and introduce noise)
Prompt engineering is not needed for large models (even the largest models benefit significantly from well-crafted prompts)

name: Advanced Prompting Strategies
description: Advanced prompting methods including structured output, self-consistency, and prompt chaining
key_facts: Self-consistency generates multiple reasoning paths and selects the most common answer
Prompt chaining breaks complex tasks into sequential subtasks with intermediate outputs
Structured output formatting (JSON, XML) can be enforced through careful prompt design
ReAct prompting combines reasoning traces with action steps for tool-using agents
Top-k and top-p (nucleus) sampling control the diversity of generated outputs
common_misconceptions: Self-consistency requires a single correct reasoning path (it works by sampling multiple paths and taking majority vote)
Structured output prompts guarantee valid JSON (output parsing and validation are still needed)

name: RAG Pipeline Fundamentals
description: Core concepts of Retrieval-Augmented Generation including document chunking, embedding, retrieval, and generation
key_facts: RAG combines information retrieval with text generation to ground LLM responses in external knowledge
Documents are split into chunks, embedded into vectors, and stored in a vector database for similarity search
Cosine similarity and dot product are common metrics for matching query embeddings to document embeddings
NVIDIA NeMo Retriever provides optimized embedding and retrieval microservices for RAG
Chunk size and overlap significantly affect retrieval quality and generation accuracy
common_misconceptions: RAG eliminates all hallucinations (it reduces them but the model can still generate inaccurate information from retrieved context)
Larger chunks always provide better context (overly large chunks dilute relevant information and can exceed context limits)

name: Vector Databases and Embedding Models
description: Vector storage solutions and embedding models used in RAG pipelines
key_facts: Vector databases like Milvus, FAISS, and Pinecone store high-dimensional embeddings for fast similarity search
Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) algorithms like HNSW and IVF trade exactness for speed
Embedding models convert text into fixed-size dense vectors that capture semantic meaning
NVIDIA NeMo Retriever Embedding provides GPU-accelerated embedding generation
Hybrid search combines dense vector retrieval with sparse keyword matching (BM25) for better results
common_misconceptions: All embedding models produce vectors of the same dimension (dimensions vary by model, e.g., 384, 768, 1024)
Vector databases are just regular databases with a vector column (they use specialized indexing algorithms optimized for similarity search)

name: LoRA and Parameter-Efficient Fine-tuning
description: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods including LoRA, QLoRA, and adapter-based approaches
key_facts: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) adds small trainable rank-decomposition matrices to frozen pre-trained weights
QLoRA combines 4-bit quantization of the base model with LoRA for memory-efficient fine-tuning
PEFT methods train less than 1% of total parameters while achieving results close to full fine-tuning
Adapter layers insert small neural network modules between existing transformer layers
NVIDIA NeMo Framework supports LoRA, P-tuning, and adapter-based fine-tuning for LLMs
common_misconceptions: LoRA changes the original model weights (it freezes them and adds separate low-rank matrices)
PEFT always matches full fine-tuning quality (for some tasks, full fine-tuning still outperforms PEFT methods)

name: TensorRT-LLM and Inference Optimization
description: NVIDIA TensorRT-LLM for optimizing LLM inference performance on GPUs
key_facts: TensorRT-LLM compiles and optimizes LLM models for high-performance GPU inference
In-flight batching (continuous batching) processes new requests without waiting for an entire batch to complete
KV cache optimization reduces redundant computation by caching key-value pairs from previous tokens
Quantization (FP8, INT8, INT4) reduces model memory footprint and increases inference throughput
Tensor parallelism splits model layers across multiple GPUs for serving large models
common_misconceptions: Quantization always significantly degrades model quality (modern quantization techniques like FP8 preserve most accuracy)
TensorRT-LLM only works with NVIDIA proprietary models (it supports open-source models like LLaMA, Mistral, and GPT-J)

name: NVIDIA NIM and Triton Inference Server
description: NVIDIA inference microservices and Triton Inference Server for deploying LLMs in production
key_facts: NVIDIA NIM (NVIDIA Inference Microservices) provides pre-built optimized containers for deploying AI models
Triton Inference Server supports multiple frameworks (TensorRT, PyTorch, ONNX, TensorFlow) simultaneously
Triton supports dynamic batching, model ensembles, and concurrent model execution
NIM containers include TensorRT-LLM optimization and expose OpenAI-compatible API endpoints
Model repositories in Triton organize model artifacts with versioning and configuration
common_misconceptions: NIM is only for cloud deployment (NIM containers can run on-premises, in the cloud, or at the edge)
Triton only serves one model at a time (it can serve multiple models concurrently with dynamic resource allocation)
